sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 2, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 1 of about 28 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The RIGHT action moved things! Let me compare before and after:
Before RIGHT:
- Green square with W center: boundary=[[30, 18], [30, 20], [32, 20], [32, 18]], center at [31, 19]
- Green square with B center: boundary=[[30, 27], [30, 29], [32, 29], [32, 27]], center at [31, 28]
- White square: boundary=[[30, 30], [30, 32], [32, 32], [32, 30]]
After RIGHT:
- Green square with W center: boundary=[[30, 21], [30, 23], [32, 23], [32, 21]], center at [31, 22] (moved right by 3)
- White square (new): boundary=[[30, 24], [30, 26], [32, 26], [32, 24]] (was at 27-29 before, now at 24-26)
- Green square with B center: boundary=[[30, 27], [30, 29], [32, 29], [32, 27]] (same position)
- White square: boundary=[[30, 30], [30, 32], [32, 32], [32, 30]] (same position)
Wait, let me re-examine. The green square with W center moved from col 18-20 to col 21-23. A new white square appeared at col 24-26. The green square with B center stayed at col 27-29, and the white square at col 30-32 stayed.
So it seems like the green square with white center (the "player") moved right by 3 columns, leaving a white square in its old position. It moved to fill the gap between where it was and the next green square.
Wait, actually looking more carefully:
- Old position of green+W: col 18-20 (center col 19)
- New position: col 21-23 (center col 22)
- The white square that was at col 24-26... wait, there was no white square there before.
Let me reconsider. Before:
- Green+W at col 18-20
- Green+B at col 27-29  
- White at col 30-32
After:
- Green+W at col 21-23
- White at col 24-26
- Green+B at col 27-29
- White at col 30-32
So the green+W moved right by 3 cells, and a white square appeared at col 24-26. It seems like the green+W square "jumped" over a white square that appeared in between.
Actually wait - looking at the initial state again:
Row 30: `wwwwwwwwNNNwwwwwwNNNwwwppppppwwwwwcccccwwwww`
Row 31: `wwwwwwwwNWNwwwwwwNBNwwwppppppwwwwwwwwwwwww`
Row 32: `wwcccccwwNNNwwwwwwNNNwwwppppppwwwwwwwwwwwww`
The green+N squares are at col 18-20 and col 27-29. Between them at row 30-32, cols 21-26 there was white (w) area.
After RIGHT:
- Green+W moved from col 18-20 to col 21-23
- White square appeared at col 24-26
So the green+W square moved right by 3 columns. The space it left behind... let me check if the dark gray square below it changed.
The dark gray square at row 32-36, col 11-15 seems unchanged. But wait, looking at the new boundaries:
- id=12 (c): boundary=[[32, 11], [32, 15], [36, 15], [36, 11]] - same
- id=13 (w): boundary=[[33, 12], [33, 14], [35, 14], [35, 12]] - same
So the dark gray square didn't move. The green+W square moved right.
It seems like there's a "player" piece (the green square with white center) that can be moved with arrow keys. The goal might be to move it to a specific location, perhaps into the dark gray frame on the right.
Let me try more RIGHT actions to move the green+W square toward the right panel.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: World model: The green square with white center (at col 18-20) is the player piece. RIGHT moves it right by 3 columns, leaving a white trail. The goal likely involves moving this piece to the dark gray frame on the right panel.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me try moving RIGHT several times to get to the right panel
# The green+W is now at col 21-23, need to get to col 44-48 area
# Each RIGHT moves 3 columns. Need to move from ~col 22 to ~col 46, about 24 columns = 8 moves
# But there's a green+B at col 27-29 and purple bar at col 33-38

# Let me try a few RIGHTs and see what happens
result = action(['RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT'])
print("Result:", result)

seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
print("\nSmall nodes after 3x RIGHT:")
for n in nodes:
    if n['pixels'] <= 20:
        print(f"  id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
